title: Treehouse Of Horror Vi

jokes.text: Ah, the Miracle Mile, where value wears a neon sombrero... and there's not a single church or library to offend the eye.
jokes.setup: Homer's appreciation of a commercial strip
jokes.punchline: Churches and libraries would 'offend the eye' - inverting normal values
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character-specific satire - Homer's values perfectly inverted. Sharp writing with visual metaphor ('neon sombrero')

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You know, if they made a strip of bacon, I'd watch that.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, that's disgusting.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: What? It's just bacon. Although, we'd have to censor the churches and libraries - they'd offend the eye.

jokes.text: There it is! The chain that put the 'fat' in fat Southern sheriffs!
jokes.setup: Homer spotting Lard Lad Donuts
jokes.punchline: Clever wordplay linking the chain to fat sheriffs stereotype
jokes.explanation: Solid wordplay with cultural reference, fits Homer's character perfectly

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Lard Lad Donuts... Now there's a donut shop named after me!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait, that's not right. It should be 'Homer Lad Donuts.'

jokes.text: D'oh! Nuts! That's false advertising!
jokes.setup: Homer discovering the donut sign isn't actual size
jokes.punchline: D'oh + Nuts = donuts, plus his outrage at false advertising
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer catchphrase twisted into donut pun, plus his typical misplaced righteous indignation

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh! Nuts equals donuts? False advertising!

jokes.text: Eggheads. What do they know?
jokes.setup: Scientists warning about ionic disturbance
jokes.punchline: Homer dismissing scientists as 'eggheads' who don't know anything
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer anti-intellectualism, though predictable

dialogue.speaker: Scientist
dialogue.line: We've detected an ionic disturbance that could be catastrophic.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Eggheads! You don't know anything. I'm not listening to a bunch of nerds.

jokes.text: Hee-hee-hee! I got your doughnut, Lard Lad! And what are you gonna do about it?
jokes.setup: Homer taunting an inanimate mascot statue
jokes.punchline: His childish glee at 'stealing' from a statue
jokes.explanation: Homer's childish triumph, sets up the supernatural revenge plot

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Ha ha! I'm stealing from a statue! Woo-hoo!

jokes.text: Well, I acquired it legally. You can be sure of that.
jokes.setup: Marge asking about the giant donut
jokes.punchline: Homer's transparently false claim of legality
jokes.explanation: Homer's terrible lying, audience knows he stole it

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, where did you get that giant donut?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I found it.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: You found it? Homer, that's a three-foot donut from Lard Lad Donuts!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I know what I found, Marge. And I looked it up on the internet — finders keepers is still a valid legal doctrine. I checked three websites.

jokes.text: Dude, you're huge! Yeah! Let's party, baby! Hey-hey-hey!
jokes.setup: Kids reacting to giant advertising mascots
jokes.punchline: They're excited rather than terrified by the monsters
jokes.explanation: Kids' inappropriate excitement at destruction fits their characters

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Whoa! Check out the size of that thing!

dialogue.speaker: Milhouse
dialogue.line: It's so big and scary!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Scary? Are you kidding? This is awesome!

dialogue.speaker: Milhouse
dialogue.line: Yeah, yeah! I want one of those for my room!

jokes.text: Perhaps it's part of some daring new ad campaign. But what new product could justify such carnage? A cleanser? A fat-free fudge cake that doesn't let you down in the flavor department like so many others-
jokes.setup: News anchor trying to rationalize monster attack
jokes.punchline: Suggesting mass destruction is just advertising, then getting carried away with product pitch
jokes.explanation: Brilliant media satire - newscaster can't help but pitch products even during disaster coverage

dialogue.speaker: Kent Brockman
dialogue.line: Well, it appears we're experiencing what insurance companies call an 'act of God.' Or in this case, an act of... a really big monster. But let's look at the bright side - all this destruction is really just free advertising! Why, I haven't seen this much excitement since the opening of the new Kwik-E-Mart!

dialogue.speaker: Kent Brockman
dialogue.line: Speaking of which, the Kwik-E-Mart: your one-stop shop for all your convenience needs! Open 24 hours, featuring Squishees in five delicious flavors, and now with an expanded hot dog selection! That's the Kwik-E-Mart - because when disaster strikes, you need a snack!

jokes.text: Whoa! Another acid flashback. Man, I'd hate to be driving a bus right now!
jokes.setup: Otto seeing giant monsters attacking
jokes.punchline: He thinks it's a drug flashback and worries about driving a bus
jokes.explanation: Perfect Otto character moment - his stoner perspective makes giant monsters seem normal while worrying about his job responsibilities

dialogue.speaker: Otto
dialogue.line: Whoa! Giant monsters! That's got to be a flashback from some gnarly drugs I did back in the day.

dialogue.speaker: Otto
dialogue.line: Oh man, I'm supposed to be driving a school bus right now!

jokes.text: Chief, that wasn't a monster. That was the captain of the high school basketball team.
jokes.setup: Chief Wiggum fighting what he thinks is a monster
jokes.punchline: It was just a tall basketball player
jokes.explanation: Classic Wiggum incompetence, attacking innocent people instead of real threats

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Sweet mother of macadamia nuts! Some kind of monster!

dialogue.speaker: Lou
dialogue.line: Chief, that's just Kareem Abdul-Jabbar.

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Well, that explains the basketball uniform.

jokes.text: Uh, yeah, well, he was turning into a monster though.
jokes.setup: Being corrected about attacking a student
jokes.punchline: Wiggum's weak justification for his mistake
jokes.explanation: Wiggum's refusal to admit error, typical character beat

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Well, he looked at me funny!

jokes.text: He came to life. Good for him!
jokes.setup: Learning the Lard Lad statue came to life
jokes.punchline: Homer's bizarrely positive reaction to supernatural revenge
jokes.explanation: Homer's oblivious optimism about his supernatural nemesis is perfectly in character and absurd

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: A living Lard Lad statue? That's the greatest thing that's ever happened! Finally, a donut-based life form that understands me!

dialogue.speaker: Lard Lad
dialogue.line: I'm here to destroy you all.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Destroy us? Well, you're certainly entitled to your opinion. Can I get a dozen Boston creams before you start?

jokes.text: Well, okay. If it'll end horror.
jokes.setup: Being told to return the donut to end the monster rampage
jokes.punchline: Homer's reluctant agreement, as if it's a minor inconvenience
jokes.explanation: Homer treating city-wide destruction as a minor annoyance

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Okay, okay, I'll return the donut.

jokes.text: Don't you ever get tired of being wrong all the time? Sometimes.
jokes.setup: Marge's exasperated question about Homer being wrong
jokes.punchline: Homer's honest, understated admission
jokes.explanation: Perfect marriage comedy - Homer's casual admission to chronic wrongness is both honest and absurd

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, you're wrong about this, aren't you?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yep.

jokes.text: And you kids should have jackets on.
jokes.setup: Marge commenting on monsters destroying everything they hold dear
jokes.punchline: Her maternal concern about proper clothing during the apocalypse
jokes.explanation: Perfect Marge moment - maternal instincts overriding logical priority during crisis

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Oh, my stars! Monsters are destroying everything we hold dear!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: But at least they're wearing sensible shoes.

jokes.text: Well, sir, advertising is a funny thing. If people stop paying attention to it, pretty soon it goes away. Like that old woman who couldn't find the beef?
jokes.setup: Explaining how to stop the advertising monsters
jokes.punchline: Reference to forgotten Wendy's commercial ('Where's the beef?')
jokes.explanation: Meta-commentary on how advertising fades from memory, using specific cultural reference

dialogue.speaker: Ad Executive
dialogue.line: To stop the advertising monsters, you have to remember the forgotten commercial. You know, 'Where's the beef?'

jokes.text: Don't watch the mon- Don't watch the... monsters-s-s-s
jokes.setup: Trying to create a jingle about not watching monsters
jokes.punchline: His inability to make it work, stuttering and failing
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy of failed jingle creation, sets up Paul Anka solution

dialogue.speaker: Ad Executive
dialogue.line: Okay, let's see... 'Don't watch the monsters, they're bad for your... your...'

dialogue.speaker: Ad Executive
dialogue.line: 'They're bad for your... uh... your...' No, no, this isn't working.

dialogue.speaker: Ad Executive
dialogue.line: 'Don't, don't watch the mon-mon-monsters...' Ugh, forget it.

jokes.text: Well, it'll sound a lot better coming out of Paul Anka.
jokes.setup: Failed jingle attempt
jokes.punchline: Solution is to get celebrity singer Paul Anka
jokes.explanation: Absurd solution to use random celebrity, typical Simpsons celebrity cameo setup

dialogue.speaker: Ad Executive
dialogue.line: We need a fresh approach. Get me Paul Anka.

dialogue.speaker: Paul Anka
dialogue.line: Hey, I'll write you a jingle. But first, I gotta practice a little.

dialogue.speaker: Ad Executive
dialogue.line: Just make it catchy and memorable.

dialogue.speaker: Paul Anka
dialogue.line: You like seafood, don't ya? Well, have I got news for you!

jokes.text: To stop those monsters one, two, three / Here's a fresh new way that's trouble-free / It's got Paul Anka's guarantee / Guarantee void in Tennessee
jokes.setup: Paul Anka's solution jingle
jokes.punchline: Bizarre legal disclaimer that guarantee is void in Tennessee
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurd detail - why would a monster-stopping guarantee not work in Tennessee? Perfect Simpsons random specificity

dialogue.speaker: Paul Anka
dialogue.line: ♪ You tried your best and you failed miserably. The lesson is: never try. ♪

dialogue.speaker: Announcer
dialogue.line: This guarantee is void in Tennessee.

jokes.text: It worked! They're all dead! Well, except for chubsy-ubsy over there.
jokes.setup: Celebrating the success of not looking at monsters
jokes.punchline: Casual, cute nickname for the one remaining monster (Lard Lad)
jokes.explanation: Paul Anka's affectionate nickname for the donut monster undermines the threat

dialogue.speaker: Paul Anka
dialogue.line: Hey Lard Lad, you're not so bad when I'm not looking at you. From now on, I'm gonna call you... Lard Pal.

jokes.text: Mmm. Sprinkles! Homer, stop looking! Don't make us poke your eyes out, Dad.
jokes.setup: Homer should stop looking at the monster
jokes.punchline: He's distracted by sprinkles, family threatens eye violence
jokes.explanation: Homer's food obsession overrides survival instinct, family's casual violence threat

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, stop looking at the monster!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: But Marge, he's covered in sprinkles!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, we're gonna poke your eyes out.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Mm-hmm.

jokes.text: Oh, shazbatt!
jokes.setup: Homer realizing he's in trouble in space
jokes.punchline: Made-up alien swear word
jokes.explanation: Mild humor from made-up alien profanity, fits space setting

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: Good catch, boy. Thanks, Bart.
jokes.setup: Playing fetch with the dog
jokes.punchline: The dog talks back and thanks him
jokes.explanation: Surreal moment where family dog politely responds to Homer, dream logic absurdity

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Here boy! Fetch!

dialogue.speaker: Santa's Little Helper
dialogue.line: Thanks, Homer!

jokes.text: Glad to 'rake' your acquaintance.
jokes.setup: Willie appearing in Bart's nightmare with a rake
jokes.punchline: Pun on 'make your acquaintance' with rake weapon
jokes.explanation: Serviceable pun for horror villain, fits Willie's character and weapon choice

dialogue.speaker: Willie
dialogue.line: I'm gonna rake you!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: That's not funny!

dialogue.speaker: Willie
dialogue.line: Aye, but I'm pleased to make your acquaintance!

jokes.text: It was only a dream. Bart! Is that you? Yes! Take out the garbage.
jokes.setup: Bart relieved it was just a nightmare
jokes.punchline: Immediately gets mundane chore from dad, deflating the relief
jokes.explanation: Good structural joke - nightmare relief immediately replaced by mundane reality

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Phew! It was just a nightmare!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Bart, go clean out the gutters.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: Groundskeeper Willie! Oh, my god!
jokes.setup: Bart describing his nightmare attacker
jokes.punchline: Lisa's shock at recognizing the 'mysteriously disappeared' janitor
jokes.explanation: Decent reaction beat that advances the plot while building suspense

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: There was this guy, right? He had a mop, and these dead eyes, and he kept coming at me saying 'You made a mess, boy. You made a mess.'

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Bart, that's just Willie. He mysteriously disappeared three weeks ago.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Willie? My nightmare guy is Willie?

jokes.text: Now, let's have no more curiosity about this bizarre cover-up.
jokes.setup: Trying to discourage investigation into Willie's disappearance
jokes.punchline: Accidentally admits it's a cover-up while denying it
jokes.explanation: Perfect Skinner incompetence - trying to hide something while explicitly naming it a cover-up

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: There's nothing to investigate here. Willie simply took a personal day. We don't need to cover anything up.

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: And even if we were covering something up, which we're not, it would be perfectly justified given the circumstances.

jokes.text: Remember, class, the worse you do on this standardized test... the more funding the school gets, so don't knock yourselves out.
jokes.setup: Instructions for taking a standardized test
jokes.punchline: Admits the perverse incentive system where failure gets more money
jokes.explanation: Sharp satire of education funding systems where schools benefit from poor performance - still relevant decades later

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: Now children, you all know how important these standardized tests are. If you fail, the school gets more federal funding.

jokes.text: I am the Wondrous Wizard of Latin! I am a dervish of declension and a conjurer of conjugation... with a million hit points and maximum charisma!
jokes.setup: Martin getting excited about Latin class
jokes.punchline: Mixing academic enthusiasm with RPG gaming terminology
jokes.explanation: Perfect Martin characterization - nerdy academic passion combined with gaming culture references

dialogue.speaker: Martin
dialogue.line: Ah, Latin class! The most exquisite language of the ancient world! I simply cannot contain my enthusiasm!

dialogue.speaker: Martin
dialogue.line: The declensions, the conjugations — they grant me a +15 bonus to my Intelligence stat!

dialogue.speaker: Martin
dialogue.line: Soon I shall achieve max level in philological mastery and unlock the achievement: 'Grammaticus Maximus!'

jokes.text: You've mastered a dead tongue! But can you handle a live one?
jokes.setup: Martin showing off Latin skills
jokes.punchline: Willie's pun about dead language vs. live tongue (his weapon)
jokes.explanation: Clever pun connecting academic achievement to horror threat, fits Willie's rough intelligence

dialogue.speaker: Martin
dialogue.line: Latin is a dead language, but it lives on through speakers like me.

dialogue.speaker: Willie
dialogue.line: Aye, and me tongue's a live weapon that'll kill ye just the same!

jokes.text: Wheel him out quietly. It's best the children don't see him.
jokes.setup: Martin has been killed by Willie
jokes.punchline: Treating student death as minor administrative inconvenience
jokes.explanation: Dark humor - Skinner's bureaucratic response to student death

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: Well, I'm afraid we'll need to file some paperwork. Martin's death will require us to update our student roster, notify the district, and... oh, we'll also need to assign his locker to another student by next week.

jokes.text: Um, we didn't mention Groundskeeper Willie, Mom.
jokes.setup: Marge says she doesn't see what it has to do with Willie
jokes.punchline: Lisa points out they never mentioned Willie
jokes.explanation: Lisa's logic catching Marge's suspicious knowledge, advances plot efficiently

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: That's exactly my point. We never mentioned Willie.

jokes.text: Lousy 'Smarch' weather.
jokes.setup: Reference to the 13th month mentioned earlier
jokes.punchline: Willie complaining about weather in made-up month 'Smarch'
jokes.explanation: Became one of the most quoted Simpsons lines ever - absurd made-up month name with perfect Willie delivery, callbacks to misprinted calendars

dialogue.speaker: Willie
dialogue.line: Ach! It's too hot in Smarch! And too cold! And there's a storm of chocolate every other day!

jokes.text: You'll pay for this with your children's blood!
jokes.setup: Willie burning to death while school board ignores him
jokes.punchline: His over-the-top revenge threat
jokes.explanation: Standard horror villain threat, fits Willie's character and situation

dialogue.speaker: Willie
dialogue.line: Och! I'm burnin' to death! Help me! Please!

dialogue.speaker: School Board Member
dialogue.line: Shh, he's still talking.

dialogue.speaker: Willie
dialogue.line: When I get outta here, I'm gonna kill ye all!

jokes.text: Oh, right. How you gonna get 'em? Skeleton power?
jokes.setup: Willie's supernatural revenge threat
jokes.punchline: Barney's drunk logic questioning ghost powers
jokes.explanation: Perfect Barney moment - drunk guy logically questioning supernatural threats with naive terminology

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: Wait a minute. If Willie's a ghost, how come he can touch things? Ghosts can't touch things.

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: Oh wait, I'm drunk.

jokes.text: Bah! Welcome to my world.
jokes.setup: Kids realizing they could die if they fall asleep
jokes.punchline: Grandpa's casual response implying he lives with that threat
jokes.explanation: Perfect Grandpa Simpson line - elderly person's casual relationship with mortality threat

dialogue.speaker: Grandpa Simpson
dialogue.line: What's the big deal? I've been living with that threat since I turned 50.

jokes.text: Okay. But promise you won't be grouchy.
jokes.setup: Bart asking Lisa to wake him if he's in trouble during the dream fight
jokes.punchline: Lisa's concern about Bart's mood after potentially saving his life
jokes.explanation: Good sibling dynamic - petty concerns during life-or-death situation

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Lisa, if I'm in trouble, wake me up, okay?

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Okay.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Bart! Bart, wake up! You're in trouble!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: What? What happened?

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: You were fighting that guy and losing, so I woke you up.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Oh, thanks.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Are you okay? You seem kind of quiet.

jokes.text: When I'm done with you, they'll have to do a 'compost-mortem!'
jokes.setup: Willie threatening Bart in dream
jokes.punchline: Pun on 'post-mortem' with composting/gardening theme
jokes.explanation: Solid pun that connects Willie's groundskeeper job to death threat

dialogue.speaker: Willie
dialogue.line: Ach! I'll catch ye, ye wee bampot, and when I do, it'll be a post-mortem examination!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Post-mortem? Willie, I think you mean post-composting!

dialogue.speaker: Willie
dialogue.line: Aye, that's what I said! Now hold still while I tend to yer eternal rest in me compost heap!

jokes.text: Listen up. It's your basic Statue of Liberty play with one twist. You throw it to me. Knute Rockne called it 'the forward pass.'
jokes.setup: Krusty explaining football strategy in Bart's dream
jokes.punchline: Claiming the forward pass was invented by Knute Rockne as his innovation
jokes.explanation: Dream logic absurdity - Krusty as football coach giving historically inaccurate sports facts

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: You see, kid, the forward pass was invented by Knute Rockne as his innovation.

jokes.text: I hope you get reincarnated as someone who can stay awake for 15 minutes.
jokes.setup: Bart and Lisa saying goodbye as they face death
jokes.punchline: Bart's specific insult about Lisa's inability to stay awake
jokes.explanation: Bart managing to be mean to Lisa even in their final moments, very in character

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Well, Lis, I guess this is it.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I know. At least we're together.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Yeah, but you're gonna fall asleep before we even die.

jokes.text: Boo!
jokes.setup: Lisa saying Willie could come back any time
jokes.punchline: Willie immediately proves her right with simple 'boo'
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective horror movie ending - immediate proof of the warning

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: You could come back any time.

dialogue.speaker: Willie
dialogue.line: Boo!

jokes.text: Nu-uh, because we called it. Did not. Well, we're calling it now. You are? 'Fraid so. Ohh! They got me with their legal mumbo jumbo!
jokes.setup: Homer trying to claim the closet hiding spot
jokes.punchline: Kids using 'calling it' rules, Homer defeated by kid logic
jokes.explanation: Homer being outsmarted by children's playground rules, treats it as complex legal maneuvering

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm calling the closet! It's the perfect hiding spot!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: You can't call it, Dad. We called it first.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Yeah, the rules are clear. Whoever calls it first gets it.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: But I'm the father! I should get first dibs!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, that's not how 'calling it' works.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well... I'm calling being the father!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: You can't call that either. You were born that way.

jokes.text: It's like something out of that twilight-y show about that zone.
jokes.setup: Homer discovering the mysterious portal
jokes.punchline: Can't remember 'Twilight Zone' properly
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer cultural reference failure, can't quite remember famous show title

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line:
Whoa! What is this thing? Some kind of weird portal?
Wait, I've seen this before... it's like that show...
You know, the one with the spooky music and the guy narrating?
*dun dun dun dun* ...
Oh man, what was it called? 'The Scary Zone'? 'The Weird Dimension'?
No wait... 'The Confused Zone'?
Argh! It's on the tip of my tongue!
It's definitely not 'The Twilight Zone'... or is it?

jokes.text: You're gonna help us clean and organize them. And pry out all the dead hermit crabs. Get a screwdriver.
jokes.setup: Patty and Selma have a family activity planned
jokes.punchline: Increasingly gross details about their shell collection project
jokes.explanation: Perfect Patty and Selma activity - disgusting and boring, escalating unpleasantness

dialogue.speaker: Patty
dialogue.line: Well, Selma, I found three more shells down by the pier.

dialogue.speaker: Selma
dialogue.line: Wonderful! I've been soaking ours in vinegar to get the smell out.

dialogue.speaker: Patty
dialogue.line: The smell? Selma, there's still a crab in that one.

dialogue.speaker: Selma
dialogue.line: Well, that explains the clicking sounds. I thought it was just settling.

dialogue.speaker: Patty
dialogue.line: It's not clicking anymore. I think it's been dead for weeks.

dialogue.speaker: Selma
dialogue.line: Even better. Dead crabs don't move around and scare you at night.

jokes.text: Oh, glory of glories! Oh, heavenly testament to the eternal majesty of God's creation! Holy macaroni!
jokes.setup: Homer entering the 3D dimension
jokes.punchline: Over-the-top religious language ending with 'Holy macaroni'
jokes.explanation: Homer's awe expressed in grandiose then silly terms, escalation from profound to silly

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Holy macaroni!

jokes.text: Well, as the tree said to the lumberjack, I'm stumped.
jokes.setup: Wiggum trying to figure out where Homer went
jokes.punchline: Elaborate setup for simple 'stumped' pun
jokes.explanation: Typical Wiggum over-elaborate pun setup, fits his character's tendency toward bad jokes

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Where could Homer Simpson have gone? I've checked every donut shop, every bar, every place a man like that would logically be...

jokes.text: It's like he just disappeared into fat air.
jokes.setup: Continuing to explain Homer's disappearance
jokes.punchline: Malapropism: 'fat air' instead of 'thin air'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Wiggum malapropism that actually makes sense given Homer's weight, became a quotable line

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: He just disappeared into thin air.

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Or as I like to call it, fat air.

jokes.text: Did anyone see the movie Tron? No. No. No. No. No. Yes. Er, um, I mean, no. No.
jokes.setup: Homer trying to explain the 3D dimension through movie reference
jokes.punchline: Everyone says no except one person who quickly changes their answer
jokes.explanation: Good crowd comedy - one person admitting to seeing unpopular movie then backtracking

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You know that third dimension they use in movies? Well, that's what we're living in!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Maggie
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Yes! Wait, no.

jokes.text: This forms... a three-dimensional object known as a cube... or a 'Frinkahedron,' in honor of its discoverer.
jokes.setup: Frink explaining three-dimensional geometry
jokes.punchline: Claiming to have discovered the cube and naming it after himself
jokes.explanation: Perfect Frink ego moment - claiming to discover something that's existed forever. Great character work

dialogue.speaker: Professor Frink
dialogue.line: I've discovered a three-dimensional object with six equal square faces! I'm naming it the Frink-ahedron!

jokes.text: This forms... a three-dimensional object known as a cube... or a 'Frinkahedron,' in honor of its discoverer.
jokes.setup: Frink explaining three-dimensional geometry
jokes.punchline: Claims to have discovered/invented the cube
jokes.explanation: Brilliant academic ego joke - Frink naming the basic cube after himself as if he invented it

dialogue.speaker: Professor Frink
dialogue.line: I've discovered the cube! It's a three-dimensional square!

jokes.text: Enough of your borax, Poindexter! A man's life is at stake! We need action!
jokes.setup: Frink giving scientific explanation
jokes.punchline: Wiggum's anti-intellectual outburst with old-fashioned insults
jokes.explanation: Wiggum's impatience with science, using 'borax' as dismissive term and 'Poindexter' as nerd insult

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Oh, pipe down, Frink! All this science mumbo-jumbo is making my head hurt. Back in my day, we didn't need all these fancy words and equations. A man was a man, and he didn't need to understand nothin' about nothin'!

jokes.text: Oh, there's so much I don't know about astrophysics. I wish I read that book by that wheelchair guy.
jokes.setup: Wiggum realizing his scientific ignorance
jokes.punchline: Can't remember Stephen Hawking's name, refers to him as 'wheelchair guy'
jokes.explanation: Classic Wiggum cultural ignorance - knows Hawking exists but can't remember his name

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: That wheelchair guy, you know, the one who talks through the computer? He's real smart.

jokes.text: Crap! Crap! Crap! Crap! Crap! Crap! Crap! Crap! Crap! Crap!
jokes.setup: Homer falling through the 3D dimension toward a black hole
jokes.punchline: Repeated simple profanity as his only response to cosmic terror
jokes.explanation: Homer's basic vocabulary failing him in existential crisis, repetition builds the panic

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh! D'oh! D'oh! D'oh! D'oh!

jokes.text: D'oh! Eew! This is the worst place yet.
jokes.setup: Homer lands somewhere after falling through dimensions
jokes.punchline: Even worse than cosmic void - it's real world
jokes.explanation: Meta joke - after cosmic adventure, reality is the worst place, fits show's cynicism

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Even worse than cosmic void - it's real world

jokes.text: Ooh! Erotic cakes.
jokes.setup: Homer sees something that catches his interest in the real world
jokes.punchline: Immediately distracted by sexual bakery items
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer ending - after dimensional travel, immediately focused on food and sex

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Mmm... donuts.

tmdb_overview: In "Attack of the 50 Foot Eyesores," strange atmospheric conditions bring giant advertising statues to life. In "Nightmare on Evergreen Terrace," Groundskeeper Willie is killed in a freak accident and seeks revenge in the childrens' dreams. In the final segment, Homer steps through a secret portal and becomes three-dimensional.
insight: Halloween special delivers 55 jokes in 22 minutes—peak density for the era's parody episodes.
